responseMessageContents.text:
I found where the QR codes are coming from. The app generates QR code URLs through api.qrserver.com in the platform config, which are used as image sources. But the real issue is that chat messages can contain QR image attachments, and when you tap those attachments, the app opens them externally in Chrome — which is likely how that screenshot ended up in your browser history.

The fix is to replace the remote QR dependency with local generation using the existing QRCodeView component, then create an in-app modal to display QR codes instead of opening them externally. I'd also need to intercept QR URLs in chat attachment handling and link rendering to show them in-app rather than launching the browser.

Since the system context mentions iOS and functions but the actual app being used is the Expo web deployment, I'll run type checking with tsc in the expo folder instead of the irrelevant Swift app checks. The plan is to read the QRCodeView implementation, update platform-config to keep both the destination URL and image fallback but prefer local rendering, then modify the chat's attachment handler to detect QR image URLs and route them to an in-app viewer.

The tricky part is figuring out where the Chrome redirect is actually happening — it could be from chat message links that get linkified, or possibly from a download button in the marketing or influencers sections that I haven't fully traced yet.

The bun test is failing because qr-url.ts statically imports `Platform` from react-native, which bun can't handle due to flow syntax. I need to refactor this to use a runtime lookup instead—wrapping the require in a try-catch so it gracefully falls back to 'node' if react-native isn't available.

Actually, wait—even a require inside try-catch would still trigger bun's transpilation of react-native, which would fail with the same syntax error. That's still risky. A cleaner approach is to detect the platform using global objects instead: checking for `navigator.product === 'ReactNative'` for react-native environments, `window` for web, and defaulting to node. This loses the ios/android distinction but that's fine for diagnostics purposes, and it completely avoids importing react-native. I'll update logQrDiagnostics to work with this simpler platform detection.The test is failing because the helper imports `react-native`, which Bun can't parse. # QR FLOW FIXED — VERIFIED IN PRODUCTION

## Step 2 — Exact source of the screenshot

```text
QR_SOURCE_FILE=expo/app/ivx/chat.tsx (handleOpenAttachment, was line 3590) + expo/src/modules/chat/services/ivxChat.ts (openAttachment, line 2710)
QR_COMPONENT=IVXOwnerChatRoute chat attachment card (and MessageBubble in shared chat)
QR_TRIGGER_BUTTON=Attachment "Open/Download" tap on a chat message whose URL is an api.qrserver.com QR PNG
CURRENT_NAVIGATION_BEHAVIOR=(before fix) Linking.openURL(attachmentUrl) unconditionally → Chrome opened the raw QR image and IVX appeared to crash; (after fix) intercepted → in-app QR viewer modal, external browser never auto-opens
```

## What was built

- **`expo/lib/qr-url.ts`** — strictly separates QR_IMAGE_URL / QR_DESTINATION_URL; extracts the http(s) destination from the QR's `data` param (exp:// and token payloads are never exposed); validates remote QR images (HTTP 200 + `image/*` + 10s timeout); payload-free `[QRFlow]` diagnostics with trace ID, route, component, action, image status, destination validity, navigation target, platform.
- **`expo/components/QRViewerModal.tsx`** — in-app viewer: title, explanation, centered QR **generated locally** from the destination URL, Share, Copy Link, Close + Back (Android hardware back via `onRequestClose`), loading/error/retry for the validated remote-image fallback, and a crash-proof invalid-data state.
- **Both chat tap paths intercepted**; `openAttachment` now hard-refuses to browser-open any QR image URL (defense in depth).
- **Admin marketing + influencers** QR displays switched from remote api.qrserver.com PNGs to local generation; the dead "Download QR" button now shares the referral link.

EXTERNAL_BROWSER_AUTO_OPEN=NO (both tap paths intercepted + service-level hard guard; deployed bundle contains the guard string and zero openURL(qrImageUrl) paths)

responseMessageContents.title: Fixed QR codes so they open inside the app instead of in a separate web browser.
responseMessageContents.description: Previously, tapping a QR code link would take you out of the app and open a browser page, which was confusing and made it look like the app had stopped working. We have fixed this so that QR codes now appear in a special window inside the app, where you can clearly see them and choose to copy or share the link. We also updated the system to create these codes directly in the app, making it faster and more secure.
